content.text: What Azure Edge Zones Are

Azure Edge Zones are small-footprint Azure deployments co-located in carrier and telco facilities at the network edge — in metropolitan areas that are geographically distant from the nearest full Azure region. They extend Azure's compute, networking, and a subset of services to locations where the round-trip latency to a full region would be too high for latency-sensitive workloads.

An Edge Zone is not an independent Azure region. It is an extension of a parent Azure region: it uses the same Azure Resource Manager endpoint, the same Azure subscription and RBAC model, the same portal, and the same management APIs. Workloads deployed in an Edge Zone are managed exactly like workloads in the parent region — they just run physically closer to end users.

attrs.alt: Azure Edge Zones: carrier co-locations extending a parent region to reduce latency for end users and 5G devices

content.text: Why Edge Zones Exist

Full Azure regions require large physical footprints: multiple datacenter buildings, hundreds of megawatts of power, and complex cooling infrastructure. This means regions are placed in metropolitan hubs with existing utility and network infrastructure. Many population centers are 50 to 500 miles from the nearest Azure region, resulting in 10 to 50ms of round-trip latency.

For most workloads, 50ms is imperceptible. But for applications like real-time gaming, video streaming from cloud-rendered environments, industrial IoT at factories, and 5G network function processing (where the mobile core must respond within 1-5ms to avoid call drops), the latency from a distant region is prohibitive.

Edge Zones solve this by placing small compute clusters inside existing carrier facilities (Equinix data halls, AT&T exchange buildings, etc.) that are already present in cities without full Azure regions. The carrier's existing power, cooling, and physical security handle the facility; Azure provides the compute, network fabric, and management plane.
Azure Edge Zones vs Full Azure Regions
Attribute
Full Azure Region
Azure Edge Zone
Physical size
Multiple buildings, hundreds of racks
Small cage or suite in a carrier co-location
Availability Zones
3 (where supported)
None — single zone only; no HA across zones
Service catalog
Full (600+ services)
Subset: VMs, AKS, Load Balancer, VNet, limited PaaS
Managed identity
Parent region
Extension of parent region; same subscription/RBAC
Distance to users
10-500ms (region-dependent)
1-5ms (carrier facility in target city)
Use case
General-purpose workloads
Ultra-low-latency: 5G, gaming, media, industrial IoT
DR/HA model
Region pairs, AZs
Failback to parent region (Edge Zone itself has no AZ protection)

Azure Edge Zones trade availability for proximity. They have no Availability Zone redundancy, so a single zone failure (power or hardware issue in the carrier facility) takes the Edge Zone offline. Always design a fallback to the parent region for resilience.
Services Available in Azure Edge Zones
Edge Zones support a curated subset of Azure services — the ones needed for compute-intensive, latency-sensitive workloads. The available services are consistent across Edge Zone locations:
Azure Virtual Machines:
standard VM SKUs (D, E, F series). GPU SKUs (NC, NV series) are available in select Edge Zones for video transcoding and AI inference workloads.
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS):
deploy containerized workloads to Edge Zone node pools. Control plane remains in the parent region.
Azure Load Balancer:
Standard SKU load balancer for distributing traffic across VMs or AKS pods in the Edge Zone.
Azure Virtual Network:
VNets peered back to the parent region, enabling communication between Edge Zone workloads and cloud-region services.
Azure ExpressRoute:
carrier-level private connectivity from enterprise or carrier networks directly into the Edge Zone.
Azure Container Instances (ACI):
serverless container execution for burst compute without managing VMs.
Azure SQL Edge:
purpose-built SQL database for edge and IoT scenarios, supporting disconnected operation.

Services NOT typically available in Edge Zones include: Azure Cosmos DB, Azure Service Bus, Azure Event Hubs, Azure Blob Storage (as a standalone service), Azure Active Directory Domain Services, and most AI/ML platform services. Workloads that need these must call back to the parent region, introducing latency for those specific calls.
Deploying to Azure Edge Zones

Deploying to an Edge Zone uses the same Azure CLI, portal, and ARM/Bicep/Terraform tooling as any other deployment. The key difference is specifying the Edge Zone's extended location when creating resources.

# List available Azure Edge Zones
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?type=='EdgeZone'].{name:name, displayName:displayName, region:metadata.pairedRegion[0].name}" \
  --output table

# Create a VNet in an Edge Zone
az network vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeVNet \
  --location eastus \
  --edge-zone "microsofteagle" \
  --address-prefix 10.1.0.0/16 \
  --subnet-name default \
  --subnet-prefix 10.1.0.0/24

# Create a VM in the Edge Zone
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeVM \
  --location eastus \
  --edge-zone "microsofteagle" \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --vnet-name myEdgeVNet \
  --subnet default \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Deploy AKS with an Edge Zone node pool
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeAKS \
  --location eastus \
  --edge-zone "microsofteagle" \
  --node-count 3 \
  --enable-managed-identity \
  --network-plugin azure
5G and Carrier-Integrated Edge Zones

A primary driver for Azure Edge Zones is 5G network function processing. 5G mobile networks require ultra-low-latency compute for functions like the User Plane Function (UPF), which routes data packets from mobile devices to the internet. These functions must process packets in under 5ms to meet 5G latency targets; any latency to a cloud region adds to the packet processing time and degrades quality of service.

Carriers (AT&T, Verizon, Vodafone, and others) co-host Azure Edge Zones inside their central office buildings where the 5G radio access network (RAN) equipment terminates. This places Azure compute inside the same building as the 5G core, eliminating the round-trip to a cloud region for UPF processing.

# Example: Deploy a 5G User Plane Function (UPF) container on an Edge Zone AKS node pool
# (Conceptual; actual UPF image is provided by the telco or NFVI vendor)

# Create an AKS Edge Zone node pool with DPDK-capable VMs for packet processing
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myEdgeAKS \
  --name upfpool \
  --node-count 2 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_F16s_v2 \
  --node-taints "workload=upf:NoSchedule"

# Deploy UPF workload with node selector for the Edge Zone pool
# (This is a kubectl manifest; the control plane is in the parent region)
# kubectl apply -f upf-deployment.yaml
# where upf-deployment.yaml includes:
# nodeSelector:
#   agentpool: upfpool
#   topology.kubernetes.io/zone: eastus-edgezone-microsofteagle
Network Connectivity Between Edge Zone and Parent Region

Edge Zones connect to their parent region via Microsoft's private WAN backbone, not the public internet. This means traffic between your Edge Zone workloads and services in the parent region (databases, AI APIs, storage) travels over a low-latency, high-bandwidth private network. The typical latency between an Edge Zone and its parent region is 10 to 30ms depending on geographic distance.

# Peer an Edge Zone VNet back to the parent region VNet
az network vnet peering create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name EdgeToRegion \
  --vnet-name myEdgeVNet \
  --remote-vnet /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myRegionVNet \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Reverse peering
az network vnet peering create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name RegionToEdge \
  --vnet-name myRegionVNet \
  --remote-vnet /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myEdgeVNet \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Verify peering state
az network vnet peering show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myEdgeVNet \
  --name EdgeToRegion \
  --query peeringState
Typical Architecture: Latency-Sensitive Application

A media streaming platform serving live game video to stadium audiences or mobile gamers is a good example of an Edge Zone use case. The video transcoding pipeline runs in the Edge Zone for low-latency delivery; the content management system, user accounts, and analytics run in the parent Azure region.
User devices connect to an Azure Public IP in the Edge Zone via CDN or direct connection.
An AKS pod in the Edge Zone performs real-time video transcoding (GPU-accelerated, sub-50ms latency to the stadium's local feed).
Transcoded video is packaged and sent to the CDN edge node (also physically in or near the carrier facility).
The AKS control plane, user account APIs, and analytics data lake run in the parent Azure region.
The Edge Zone AKS nodes communicate with parent region services (identity, storage, databases) over the private peered VNet connection.
If the Edge Zone has an outage, traffic manager redirects to a fallback stream from the parent region with higher latency.

# Bicep: Deploy resources spanning Edge Zone and parent region
# (Simplified; shows the extended location pattern)

# Edge Zone VM (bicep pseudocode structure equivalent)
# resource edgeVM 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines@2023-03-01' = {
#   name: 'myEdgeVM'
#   location: 'eastus'
#   extendedLocation: {
#     type: 'EdgeZone'
#     name: 'microsofteagle'
#   }
#   ...
# }

# Terraform: azure edge zone resource
# resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine" "edge" {
#   name                = "myEdgeVM"
#   resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
#   location            = "eastus"
#   edge_zone           = "microsofteagle"
#   size                = "Standard_D4s_v3"
#   ...
# }
Availability and Resilience Considerations

Azure Edge Zones are designed for latency, not high availability. They run in a single physical location with no Availability Zone protection. Design your application to tolerate Edge Zone outages by:
Active/standby with parent region:
run the latency-critical path in the Edge Zone; have a standby deployment in the parent region that activates automatically via Traffic Manager or Front Door when the Edge Zone fails.
Stateless Edge workloads:
keep state (user data, session state, media files) in the parent region. Edge Zone VMs and pods are ephemeral — if the facility loses power, state stored only on the Edge Zone's local disk is lost.
Health probes and failover:

configure Azure Traffic Manager or Azure Front Door with health probes pointing to both the Edge Zone endpoint and the parent region fallback. Set a short probe interval (10-30 seconds) to detect outages quickly.
RPO/RTO planning:

accept that during an Edge Zone outage, users will experience latency increase (falling back to parent region) but not loss of service. The RPO for stateless workloads is zero; the RTO depends on failover detection time.
Edge Zones vs Azure Private MEC vs Azure Stack Edge
Microsoft offers multiple edge compute products. Understanding how they differ prevents confusion when selecting the right tier for a use case:
Product
Where it runs
Who operates it
Use case
Azure Region
Microsoft-owned datacenter campus
Microsoft
General-purpose cloud workloads
Azure Edge Zone
Carrier / telco co-location facility
Microsoft (compute); Carrier (facility)
Ultra-low-latency for 5G and metropolitan users
Azure Private MEC
On-premises at enterprise or carrier site
Microsoft manages; customer/carrier hosts hardware
Private 5G, factory automation, campus edge
Azure Stack Edge (ASE)
Customer-owned premises
Customer operates the physical appliance
Air-gapped, disconnected, or hybrid edge with full Azure local
Azure Arc
Any infrastructure (on-prem, multicloud)
Customer operates; Azure manages plane
Bring Azure management to existing infrastructure

Azure Edge Zones are the best fit when your users are in a carrier-covered metropolitan area and the carrier already has an Edge Zone footprint. Azure Private MEC is better when you need a private 5G network at your own facility. Azure Stack Edge is best for disconnected or air-gapped scenarios.
Billing and Cost Model for Edge Zones

Azure Edge Zones use the same billing model as the parent region: you pay for VM compute, storage, networking, and any PaaS services consumed. There is no separate 'Edge Zone fee' — the resource costs are billed to your Azure subscription at the same rates as equivalent resources in the parent region.
However, some cost differences apply in practice:
Egress from Edge Zone:

data transferred from an Edge Zone to the public internet (to end users) is billed as egress from the parent region. Bandwidth pricing is the same; the physical path just exits at the Edge Zone location.
Cross-region traffic:
traffic between the Edge Zone and the parent region VNet (over the Microsoft backbone) is charged at the same inter-region data transfer rates as other cross-region traffic.
VM SKU availability:
not all VM SKUs are available in all Edge Zones. Check az vm list-skus for the Edge Zone's extended location before committing to a specific SKU in IaC.
No commitment discount difference:
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans purchased for the parent region apply to Edge Zone VMs of the same SKU family.

# Check available VM SKUs in a specific Edge Zone
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[?locationInfo[0].extendedLocations[?contains(@, 'microsofteagle')]].{name:name, tier:tier}" \
  --output table

# Estimate cost for Edge Zone VMs using Azure Retail Prices API
python3 << 'EOF'
import urllib.request, json

# Retail Prices API for Standard_D4s_v3 in East US
url = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&$filter=armRegionName eq 'eastus' and skuName eq 'D4s v3' and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
req = urllib.request.Request(url)
with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
    data = json.load(resp)
for item in data.get("Items", [])[:3]:
    print(f"{item['skuName']}: ${item['retailPrice']}/hour ({item['productName']})")
EOF
Monitoring Edge Zone Workloads

Azure Monitor, Application Insights, and Container Insights all work for Edge Zone workloads. Telemetry collected from Edge Zone resources is transmitted to the parent region's Log Analytics workspace over the private backbone connection. This means monitoring adds a small amount of steady-state traffic between the Edge Zone and the parent region.

# Create a Log Analytics workspace in the parent region for Edge Zone monitoring
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name myEdgeMonitorWS \
  --location eastus

# Enable Container Insights for AKS Edge Zone cluster
az aks enable-addons \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeAKS \
  --addons monitoring \
  --workspace-resource-id /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myEdgeMonitorWS

# Query latency from Edge Zone workloads (Application Insights)
# (Run in the Log Analytics query interface or via az monitor)
az monitor log-analytics query \
  --workspace myEdgeMonitorWS \
  --analytics-query "requests | where cloud_RoleName == 'myEdgeApp' | summarize avg(duration) by bin(timestamp, 5m) | order by timestamp desc" \
  --output table

# Set up an alert for latency spike from Edge Zone
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "EdgeZoneLatencyAlert" \
  --scopes /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters/myEdgeAKS \
  --condition "avg requests/duration > 10" \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --action /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG

Monitoring data latency: telemetry from an Edge Zone reaches your Log Analytics workspace with a few seconds of delay due to the network hop to the parent region. This is negligible for alerting but important to understand if you are doing real-time trace correlation.
When Not to Use Azure Edge Zones
General-purpose workloads:

if your application serves users globally or from a geographic area well-covered by a full Azure region, Edge Zones add complexity without meaningful latency benefit. Use the nearest Azure region instead.
High-availability requirements without parent region fallback:
Edge Zones have no AZ redundancy. If your SLA requires 99.99% uptime, you need a fallback architecture. If you do not want to build that, use a full Azure region with Availability Zones instead.
Services requiring full Azure PaaS catalog:

if your workload needs Cosmos DB, Service Bus, Cognitive Services, or other services not available in Edge Zones, running your compute in an Edge Zone means extra latency for every call to those services in the parent region. Run everything in the parent region instead.
Cost-optimized scenarios:
Edge Zone compute costs the same as equivalent parent region compute. If your primary concern is cost rather than latency, move workloads to a lower-cost Azure region rather than an Edge Zone.
Workloads with high data egress:

if your application generates high volumes of outbound data (large file downloads, streaming to many users globally), use Azure CDN or a multi-region deployment rather than funneling everything through a single Edge Zone.
Security Considerations for Edge Zone Deployments
Edge Zones inherit Azure's security controls, but their position at the network edge and inside third-party carrier facilities introduces additional considerations:
Physical security:

Edge Zone compute runs inside a carrier co-location facility, not a Microsoft-owned building. Microsoft manages the Azure compute hardware in a locked cage or suite, but physical access to the building is controlled by the carrier's own security policies. Regulatory frameworks that require Microsoft-exclusive physical access (some FedRAMP, ITAR scenarios) may not be satisfied by Edge Zones.
Network perimeter:

Edge Zone VMs are reachable from the internet unless protected by an NSG or Azure Firewall. Because Edge Zones are designed for low-latency public-internet delivery, the default posture is more exposed than a typical Azure region workload. Always define NSG rules to allow only the required inbound ports.
DDoS protection:

Azure DDoS Protection Standard works for Edge Zone public IP addresses. Enable it on the VNet in the Edge Zone if the service handles high-volume inbound traffic (media streaming, gaming) that is a DDoS target.
Private connectivity:

if your Edge Zone workload must not be reachable from the public internet at all (private 5G, enterprise IoT), use ExpressRoute into the Edge Zone to connect directly from the carrier's private network without assigning public IPs to your workloads.

# Create an NSG for Edge Zone VMs and restrict inbound access
az network nsg create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeNSG \
  --location eastus

# Allow only HTTPS (443) and application port (8080) inbound
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name myEdgeNSG \
  --name AllowHTTPS \
  --priority 100 \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --destination-port-ranges 443 8080 \
  --access Allow \
  --direction Inbound

# Enable DDoS Protection on the Edge Zone VNet
az network ddos-protection create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeDDoSPlan \
  --location eastus

az network vnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEdgeVNet \
  --ddos-protection true \
  --ddos-protection-plan myEdgeDDoSPlan

# Apply the NSG to the Edge Zone subnet
az network vnet subnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myEdgeVNet \
  --name default \
  --network-security-group myEdgeNSG
Edge Zones in ARM Templates and Bicep

Infrastructure-as-code for Edge Zone resources requires the `extendedLocation` property in ARM templates or the `edge_zone` attribute in Bicep and Terraform. This property is what tells the Azure Resource Manager to place the resource in the extended location rather than the parent region.

The `extendedLocation` block must appear at the resource level in ARM, or as `edge_zone = "microsofteagle"` in Terraform's `azurerm` provider (v3.x+). If you omit it, the resource deploys to the parent region, not the Edge Zone — there is no error, just silent misplacement.
Troubleshooting Common Edge Zone Issues
Symptom
Likely Cause
Resolution
Resource creation fails with 'ExtendedLocationNotSupported'
The resource type is not available in the Edge Zone
Check the Edge Zone's supported service list; move unsupported resources to parent region
VM SKU unavailable in Edge Zone
Not all SKUs are provisioned in every Edge Zone
Run az vm list-skus with --edge-zone filter; use an available SKU
High latency between Edge Zone and parent region
VNet peering not configured or traffic routing over public internet
Verify VNet peering state is 'Connected'; check route table for forced tunneling
AKS cluster creation fails with 'NodePoolNotAvailable'
Edge Zone quota or SKU constraint
Request quota increase for the Edge Zone location via Azure support
NSG rules not taking effect on Edge Zone VM
NSG applied to wrong subnet or NIC
Verify NSG is associated to the Edge Zone subnet (not the parent region subnet)
Monitoring data missing from Log Analytics workspace
Container Insights addon not deployed on Edge Zone node pool
Run az aks enable-addons with --workspace-resource-id pointing to parent region workspace
Summary

Azure Edge Zones are small Azure deployments co-located in carrier facilities to bring compute within 1-5ms of end users in metropolitan areas without full Azure regions. They are extensions of a parent region, sharing the same management plane, subscription, and APIs, but offering only a subset of Azure services and no Availability Zone redundancy.
Edge Zones are physically inside carrier co-location facilities; the carrier provides power, cooling, and physical security.
They support a subset of services: VMs, AKS, Standard Load Balancer, VNet, and limited PaaS. Full Azure services remain in the parent region.
Deployment uses standard Azure tools (CLI, portal, ARM, Terraform) with an --edge-zone parameter specifying the extended location.
Primary use cases: 5G network function processing, real-time gaming, low-latency media streaming, industrial IoT.
No Availability Zone redundancy — design a fallback to the parent region (Traffic Manager, Front Door) for resilience.
All communication between Edge Zone workloads and parent region services uses Microsoft's private backbone, not the public internet.
